title: Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS)
description: Back to 1D DP, but with an O(n^2) twist -- every position checks ALL earlier positions, plus an O(n log n) upgrade using binary search.
objectives: Define dp[i] for LIS and derive its recurrence, checking all earlier positions
Implement the O(n^2) solution and trace it by hand
Understand the O(n log n) binary-search-based improvement at a conceptual level

sections.text: Given an array, find the length of the longest SUBSEQUENCE (recall LCS's definition: order preserved, gaps allowed) where every element is strictly greater than the one before it.
The length of the longest increasing subsequence that ENDS exactly at index i.

For every index i, we check EVERY earlier index j -- if nums[j] < nums[i], then i could EXTEND whatever increasing subsequence ends at j. dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1) picks the best extension found so far. This nested loop is why the approach is O(n^2) -- similar in shape to the naive Two Pointers brute force, but here the inner comparison is genuinely necessary since ANY earlier smaller element could be the best extension point.

sections.title: Tracing dp for {10, 9, 2, 5, 3, 7, 101, 18}
steps: dp[1]: nums[1]=9. Check j=0: nums[0]=10, not < 9. dp[1] stays 1 (just {9})
dp[2]: nums[2]=2. Neither 10 nor 9 is < 2. dp[2] stays 1 (just {2})
dp[6]: nums[6]=101. Every earlier value is < 101 -- dp[6] = max over all dp[j]+1 = dp[5]+1 = 4 ({2,5,7,101} or similar)

sections.text: The O(n^2) approach re-checks every earlier index for every new element. A cleverer approach maintains a separate array (often called tails) tracking the SMALLEST possible tail value for an increasing subsequence of each length seen so far, and uses Binary Search to find where each new number fits in O(log n) instead of scanning everything.

This binary search structure is directly reused from the Binary Search lesson -- finding the leftmost position where num could be inserted to keep tails sorted. The FINAL size, not the tails array itself, is the answer -- tails does NOT necessarily contain the actual LIS elements, only tracks the pattern of best possible tail values seen so far. This is a genuinely subtle algorithm; understanding at a conceptual level (it exists, it's faster, it uses binary search) is the goal here rather than deriving it from scratch.

sections.title: O(n^2) vs O(n log n) LIS
rows: Check every earlier index for a valid extension
Maintain tails array, binary search for insertion point
Yes -- direct recurrence, similar to other DP problems here
No -- requires recognizing the tails-array trick
Needed for large inputs where O(n^2) is too slow

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting dp[i] must start at 1 for every index (every single element is trivially an increasing subsequence of length 1 by itself).
Forgetting maxLength is the answer, not dp[n-1] -- the longest increasing subsequence might not end at the LAST index at all.
Confusing the O(n log n) tails array with the actual LIS sequence -- tails only tracks best-possible tail values per length, not a real subsequence you can read off directly.
Using <= instead of < when comparing nums[j] and nums[i] -- LIS requires STRICTLY increasing values, not just non-decreasing.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What does dp[i] represent in the O(n^2) LIS solution, and what is its recurrence?
questions.answer: dp[i] is the length of the longest increasing subsequence ending exactly at index i. Its recurrence checks every earlier index j: if nums[j] < nums[i], dp[i] can potentially become dp[j] + 1, taking the maximum over all such valid j.

questions.question: Why is the final answer maxLength, not dp[n-1]?
questions.answer: The longest increasing subsequence in the whole array might end at ANY index, not necessarily the last one -- maxLength tracks the best dp[i] value seen across the entire array, which dp[n-1] alone would not capture.

questions.question: What technique does the O(n log n) LIS solution use to beat the O(n^2) approach?
questions.answer: It maintains a tails array tracking the smallest possible tail value for an increasing subsequence of each length found so far, and uses binary search to find where each new number fits in O(log n), avoiding the need to check every earlier index individually.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: LIS finds the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence in an array. The O(n^2) solution defines dp[i] as the LIS length ending at index i, checking every earlier index j and extending dp[j] + 1 whenever nums[j] < nums[i] -- the final answer is the maximum dp value across the whole array, not necessarily dp[n-1]. An O(n log n) improvement maintains a tails array of the smallest possible tail value per subsequence length, using binary search (from the Binary Search lesson) to find each new number's insertion point -- the final size of this tails array is the LIS length, though the array itself doesn't necessarily hold the actual subsequence.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: LIS dp[i] = 'longest increasing run ENDING here' -- check every earlier smaller value as a possible predecessor. The final answer is the BEST ending found anywhere, not just at the last position. Faster version: binary search finds where each number slots into the current best 'ladder' of tail values.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What does dp[i] represent in LIS's O(n^2) solution?
Why is the final answer maxLength rather than dp[n-1]?
What data structure and technique does the O(n log n) solution use?
Does the tails array in the fast solution contain the actual LIS elements?
